algorithms.title: VF/pVT Cardiac Arrest Algorithm
algorithms.description: Step-by-step management of ventricular fibrillation and pulseless ventricular tachycardia, from initial rhythm identification through defibrillation, epinephrine, antiarrhythmics, and refractory-arrest considerations.
steps: Confirm VF or pulseless VT on the cardiac monitor.
Deliver one shock immediately — biphasic 120–200 J (manufacturer-specific or escalating); monophasic 360 J.
Resume chest compressions immediately after the shock — do NOT check a pulse or rhythm first.
Continue high-quality CPR for 2 minutes (rate 100–120/min, depth 2–2.4 in, full recoil, CCF ≥60%).
Perform a rhythm check after ~2 minutes of CPR.
If VF/pVT persists: shock again (equal to or higher energy if biphasic), then immediately resume CPR.
After the second shock (or as soon as reasonably available), administer epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO during ongoing compressions; follow with a saline flush.
Continue 2-minute CPR cycles with rhythm checks, repeating epinephrine every 3–5 minutes.
If VF/pVT persists after epinephrine and continued shocks: administer amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO (first dose) OR lidocaine 1–1.5 mg/kg IV/IO during compressions.
If VF/pVT continues: second antiarrhythmic dose — amiodarone 150 mg IV/IO or lidocaine 0.5–0.75 mg/kg IV/IO.
Continue CPR-shock-epinephrine cycles; reassess and treat reversible causes (H's & T's).

If VF/pVT persists after ≥3 shocks with standard therapy: consider refractory-arrest strategies — vector-change defibrillation or dual sequential external defibrillation (DSED), and evaluate ECPR candidacy.
If an organized rhythm appears: check for a pulse — manage as ROSC or PEA as appropriate.

algorithms.title: CPR Quality Optimization During VF/pVT
algorithms.description: Key parameters and strategies for maintaining high-quality chest compressions throughout VF/pVT resuscitation.
steps: Target compression rate: 100–120 compressions per minute.
Target compression depth: 2–2.4 inches (5–6 cm) for adults.
Allow complete chest recoil between compressions — avoid leaning on the chest.
Minimize all interruptions in chest compressions — pre-shock pause should be as brief as possible.
Resume compressions immediately after shock delivery without a pulse or rhythm check.
Switch compressors every ~2 minutes, ideally timed with the rhythm check to avoid adding extra pauses.
Target chest compression fraction (CCF) ≥60%, ideally ≥80%.
Use waveform capnography after advanced airway placement: ETCO₂ 10–20 mmHg during CPR suggests adequate compressions; a sudden sustained rise may indicate ROSC.
Treat reversible causes (H's & T's) concurrently — do not defer while maintaining CPR.
